After scoring nine touchdowns on only 45 receptions in 2024, Bateman had "regression" written all over him entering last season, but it was worse than expected. The 2021 first-round pick saw his target share fall from 15.4% to a career-low 11.4% and his only double-digit fantasy effort came in Week 3. The 26-year-old remains second on Baltimore's depth chart behind Zay Flowers, but even in a new scheme and with a healthy Lamar Jackson under center, he's unlikely to produce his first top-35 fantasy campaign. Bateman isn't the most appealing late-round draft pick.

players.name: Zachariah Branch
players.outlook: Branch was selected by the Falcons in the third round of April's draft. Branch is a tiny slot receiver who posted an extremely low 5.2 aDOT during 37 collegiate games. That limited his efficiency numbers a bit, though he caught 71% of his targets and was terrific after the catch (7.9 RAC). Branch has the look of a versatile (perhaps gadget) player who will help as a rusher, slot receiver and returner. That role makes him a longshot for consistent fantasy output, but he does have a path to No. 2 receiver duties behind Drake London with the likes of Olamide Zaccheaus and Jahan Dotson his primary competition. Branch should only be drafted in dynasty.

players.name: Tre' Harris
players.outlook: Harris is a Year 2 breakout candidate after flashing in a limited role as a rookie. The 2025 second-round pick quietly played 49% of the Chargers' offensive snaps, but he spent a lot of that blocking and was limited to an 8% target share. Harris is likely to open the 2026 season behind Ladd McConkey and Quentin Johnston, but with Keenan Allen gone, his competition for No. 3 duties is 2025 fifth-round pick KeAndre Lambert-Smith and rookie Brenen Thompson. A potential starter in new playcaller Mike McDaniel's offense, the 24-year-old Harris is an intriguing late-round sleeper.

players.name: Christian Kirk
players.outlook: Kirk signed with the 49ers after spending his first eight seasons with Arizona, Jacksonville and Houston. The 2018 second-round pick peaked with a 12th-place finish in fantasy points in 2022, but that marks his only career top-25 finish and he's been way off the fantasy radar the last two seasons, partially due to injury woes (13 missed games). Kirk doesn't necessarily have a clear path to consistent targets, but with only 33-year-old Mike Evans and unproven Ricky Pearsall clearly ahead of him on the depth chart (and with George Kittle recovering from a torn Achilles), there's at least a chance he sees a fair share of Brock Purdy's targets in one of the league's most efficient offenses. The 29-year-old isn't the worst late flier in very deep leagues.

players.name: Ryan Flournoy
players.outlook: Flournoy is an intriguing deep sleeper as the favorite for No. 3 wide receiver duties behind CeeDee Lamb and George Pickens in Dallas. Flournoy flashed in 2025, putting up 14.2 fantasy PPG during the only five games in which his route participation was at least 60%. Flournoy did disappear quite a bit behind the heavily targeted Lamb and Pickens (three or fewer targets in seven of his final 10 games), so he's a long shot for standalone fantasy value even if he locks down the No. 3 gig. The 26-year-old should be added to rosters only if Lamb or Pickens are sidelined.

players.name: De'Zhaun Stribling
players.outlook: Stribling was selected by the 49ers with the 33rd overall pick in April's draft. Stribling had a productive collegiate run, catching 44-plus passes in four of his five seasons, three of which came with different schools. That production included 52-882-6 and 55-811-6 receiving lines at Oklahoma State and Ole Miss over the past two seasons. Stribling primarily worked as a perimeter receiver and peaked with a 22% target share with OSU in 2024. His overall efficiency is pedestrian, but he has good size and speed (4.36 40-yard dash at the combine) and is a plus blocker. He'll open his career behind Mike Evans and Ricky Pearsall, so consistent usage is unlikely out of the gate. He's only worth a late-round flier in deep leagues.

players.name: Jack Bech
players.outlook: Bech is a candidate for a Year 2 leap after an underwhelming rookie campaign. The 2025 second-round pick was limited to 36% of Las Vegas' offensive snaps and cleared three targets in a game only twice. He failed to find the end zone while predominantly working as a short-range target. Bech will now compete with the likes of Tre Tucker, Jalen Nailor and Dont'e Thornton Jr. for a starting gig in a new-look offense led by coach Klint Kubiak and, at some point, No. 1 overall draft pick Fernando Mendoza. The 23-year-old is likely headed for a larger role in his second season and is worth a late-round flier.

players.name: Keon Coleman
players.outlook: So far, so bust for Coleman, who has struggled to make an impact after being selected 33rd overall in the 2024 draft. After flashing in a situational deep-threat role as a rookie (15.0 aDOT), Coleman saw more high percentage looks in 2025 (11.2 aDOT). That led to a much better catch rate (from 51% to 66%), but a big dip in yardage (19.2 YPR to 10.9). Coleman has yet to emerge as a reliable, full-time starter and was even a healthy scratch at times last season. Perhaps the 23-year-old will make a Year 3 leap with Joe Brady now running the show, but he'll have his hands full overtaking DJ Moore and Khalil Shakir atop the depth chart. Coleman is only worth a late flier in deeper leagues.

players.name: Malachi Fields
players.outlook: Fields was selected by the Giants in the third round of April's draft. One of the biggest and strongest receivers in this rookie class, Fields figures to work as a perimeter, vertical target in the pros (career 13.3 aDOT during 49 collegiate games). Fields, who dropped just seven passes in college, handled a target share of, at least, 20% and air yard share of, at least, 30% during each of his final three collegiate seasons (two at Virginia, one at Notre Dame). Fields will battle Darnell Mooney, Darius Slayton and Calvin Austin III for New York's No. 2 receiver gig behind Malik Nabers. The rookie is no more than a late-round flier.

players.name: Troy Franklin
players.outlook: Midway through the 2025 season, Franklin appeared well on his way to a Year 2 breakout. The 2024 fourth-round pick entered Denver's Week 12 bye with five touchdowns and a team-high 81 targets (22% share). He was demoted during the week off, however, and ended up with just 24 total targets (a 9% share) over his final seven games, including the playoffs. In total, Franklin finished a respectable 41st among receivers in fantasy PPG, but the 23-year-old's demotion, coupled with Denver's offseason acquisition of Jaylen Waddle, means he's nowhere close to the fantasy radar. Franklin will battle Pat Bryant, Marvin Mims Jr. and Lil'Jordan Humphrey for No. 3 duties. Ignore him on draft day.

players.name: Caleb Douglas
players.outlook: Douglas was selected by the Dolphins in the third round of April's draft. Douglas is a tall, perimeter receiver with big hands and long arms who, after not seeing much work during two seasons at Florida, exploded for 60-877-6 and 54-846-7 receiving lines at Texas Tech over the last two seasons. His overall efficiency was underwhelming, including six drops and a shaky 1.81 YPRR in 2025. He impressed with a 4.39 40-yard dash at 206 pounds (111 speed score) at the combine. Douglas has a path to a large offensive role as a rookie (Malik Washington, Jalen Tolbert and fellow rookie Chris Bell are his primary competition), but life may be tough in a rebuilding Miami offense led by unproven Malik Willis. He's only worth a late flier.

players.name: Chimere Dike
players.outlook: A first-team All-Pro in his first professional season, Dike broke Tim Brown's NFL rookie record for all-purpose yards. Most of that damage came as a returner (398 yards and two TDs on punts, 1,588 yards on kicks), but Dike also produced a bit as a receiver (423 yards and four TDs) and rusher (18 yards). The versatile Dike will remain a major factor in the return game, but he has added competition at wide receiver with Calvin Ridley healthy, Carnell Tate drafted fourth overall and target hog Wan'Dale Robinson signed during free agency. The 23-year-old will handle situational short-area targets and a few carries, but he's not a fantasy option.

players.name: Skyler Bell
players.outlook: Bell was selected by the Bills in the fourth round of April's draft. Bell is an undersized, but versatile, receiver who finished 2025 second in the nation (first in this WR rookie class) in receiving yards (1,278) and, while he had a class-high 25 drops across 52 collegiate games, he was better in this area last season (five drops on 146 targets). Bell showed well at the combine, running a solid 4.40 40-yard dash and posting terrific marks in the vertical and broad jump. With DJ Moore and Khalil Shakir locked in atop Buffalo's WR depth chart, Bell will compete with Keon Coleman and Josh Palmer for work. He's only worth drafting in dynasty formats.

players.name: Pat Bryant
players.outlook: A 2025 third-round pick, Bryant played a situational role throughout his rookie season and is likely to do the same in 2026 after Denver added Jaylen Waddle as a running mate for Courtland Sutton. Bryant will battle the likes of Troy Franklin, Marvin Mims Jr. and Lil'Jordan Humphrey for No. 3 duties. During his rookie campaign, Bryant barely saw the field early on, but eventually passed Franklin on the depth chart and earned more work down the stretch. That led to more snaps but not much fantasy production, as he failed to clear 13.2 fantasy points in a single game and found the end zone once. The 23-year-old isn't on the fantasy radar right now, but he makes for a fine dynasty stash.

players.name: Xavier Legette
players.outlook: Legette is eyeing a Year 3 leap after a boom/bust start to his NFL career. The 2024 first-round pick flashed with 521 yards and four touchdowns on 55 touches as a rookie, but fell to 363 yards and three scores on 36 touches in 2025. Legette did post a pair of big games (24.2 points in Week 7 and 18.3 points in Week 11), but was otherwise held in check (3.6 PPG in the other 13 games). Legette has struggled with the ball in his hands (woeful 2.1 RAC in his career) and is behind Tetairoa McMillan and likely Jalen Coker on the Carolina WR depth chart. The 25-year-old will see some situational targets and carries, but he'll need a big Year 3 leap to find his way to fantasy relevance. He's no more than a late-round lottery ticket.

players.name: Tory Horton
players.outlook: Horton had the look of a Day 3 find by Seattle early on in 2025 prior to a season-ending shin injury suffered in Week 9. The 2025 fifth-round pick opened his career buried on the depth chart behind Jaxon Smith-Njigba and Cooper Kupp, but still found a way to make an impact while being limited to 46% of the offensive snaps. Though his production was volatile and he never cleared five targets in a game, he found the end zone five times on 23 targets. Despite the impressive showing, Horton's short-term outlook is dicey with JSN, Kupp and Rashid Shaheed presumably ahead of him on the depth chart. The 23-year-old's primary value is as a dynasty stash.

players.name: Chris Brazzell II
players.outlook: Brazzell was selected by the Panthers in the third round of April's draft. Brazzell is a tall, perimeter receiver with a big wingspan who will do most of his damage down field (career 14.2 aDOT at Tennessee) and doesn't offer much after the catch (3.6 RAC was worst in this rookie WR class). He has good speed (4.37 40-yard dash) and may settle in as a boom/bust low-volume, splash-play fantasy option. He'll begin his career battling with Jalen Coker and Xavier Legette for work behind No. 1 receiver Tetairoa McMillan. Brazzell is barely worth late-round consideration, but makes for a fine dynasty stash.

players.name: Ted Hurst III
players.outlook: Hurst was selected by the Buccaneers in the third round of April's draft. Granted he played at a small school, but Hurst ranked third in this rookie class target share (26%) and first in air yard share (45%) during his FBS career. A tall, vertical receiver, Hurst posted solid collegiate efficiency despite inconsistent QB play (only 68% of his 235 FBS targets were catchable, which is easily lowest among qualified WRs in this class). His 135-inch broad jump was best among wide receivers at the combine. Hurst will get some run at 'X' receiver as a rookie, but he's a longshot for consistent rookie-season fantasy production with Emeka Egbuka, Chris Godwin and Jalen McMillan in the fold.

players.name: Dontayvion Wicks
players.outlook: Wicks was traded to the Eagles after spending his first three NFL seasons in Green Bay. The 2023 fifth-round pick was limited to situational work during his Packers tenure, never finishing a season among the top 50 receivers in snaps, routes, targets, catches or yards. Wicks flashed at times (exactly one game of 24-plus points in each season), but otherwise scored 5.3 PPG. Even after the Eagles traded A.J. Brown, Wicks' path to targets may not be any easier in Philly. DeVonta Smith, Dallas Goedert and Saquon Barkley are all assured substantial roles, with first-round rookie Makai Lemon and Hollywood Brown also very much in the mix. Wicks, 25, will need to win the team's No. 2 receiver gig in order to have any shot at weekly flex consideration. He's barely worth late-round consideration in deep leagues.

players.name: Elijah Sarratt
players.outlook: Sarratt was selected by the Ravens in the fourth round of April's draft. Surratt is a big, perimeter receiver who hauled in 31 touchdown receptions (most in this rookie WR class) in 40 collegiate games, which includes a FBS-high 15 in 2025. His efficiency was terrific across the board, including 10.7 yards per target, 2.69 YPRR and a 72% catch rate. Surratt is an intriguing prospect, but he may not see the field a ton as a rookie with Zay Flowers, Rashod Bateman and fellow rookie Ja'Kobi Lane also in the mix in Baltimore's low-volume pass attack. He's best as a dynasty stash.

players.name: Devaughn Vele
players.outlook: Vele is battling for New Orleans' No. 3 receiver role behind Chris Olave and Jordyn Tyson following an injury-plagued first season with the team. The 2024 seventh-round pick, who posted a 41-475-3 receiving line with Denver as a rookie, barely played throughout most of last season prior to taking on an every-down role during Weeks 10-15. In those five games (one of which he left hurt), he averaged 5.6 targets per game, but reached 12.0 fantasy points once (an 8-93-1 effort in Week 13 against Miami). The 28-year-old has yet to show he can be relied on for consistent targets and fantasy production. He's well off the fantasy radar.

players.name: Jalen Tolbert
players.outlook: Tolbert signed with Miami after spending his first four NFL seasons in Dallas. The 2022 third-round pick has a path to a substantial offensive role, with Malik Washington, Tutu Atwell and rookies Chris Bell and Caleb Douglas as his top competition for snaps and targets. We have seen Tolbert as a near-full-time player in a much better offense and he wasn't much of a fantasy option. That came in 2024 when he played 76% of the Cowboys' offensive snaps. He found the end zone seven times, but was held to 610 yards and finished 54th among receivers in fantasy PPG. Tolbert played a reduced role last season and his 203 yards were the fewest since his rookie season. The 27-year-old has very limited upside and isn't worth much late-round consideration.

players.name: Hollywood Brown
players.outlook: Brown signed with the Eagles and he's likely to slot in either third or fourth on the team's depth chart behind DeVonta Smith, first-round rookie Makai Lemon and perhaps Dontayvion Wicks. Once a featured target in Baltimore and Arizona, Brown was more of a situational player during his two seasons in Kansas City. He barely saw the field in 2024 due to injury before handling a career-low 14.2% target share in 16 games in 2025. Brown cleared 10 fantasy points three times, but all came prior to Week 7. With Dallas Goedert and Saquon Barkley also heavily in the mix for targets in Philly, Brown will have to beat out Lemon and Wicks for a starting gig in order to have any chance at flex value.

players.name: Savion Williams
players.outlook: Williams is primed for a larger role in his second NFL season. The 2025 third-round pick had a unique rookie campaign, handling more carries (11) than he did targets (10). Williams' gadget role allowed him only 90 snaps (12%) and he was utilized only near the line of scrimmage (1.4 aDOT). The Green Bay WR rotation no longer includes Romeo Doubs or Dontayvion Wicks, but Christian Watson, Jayden Reed and fellow second-year wideout Matthew Golden remain in the mix. Williams will need to beat out one (or perhaps two) of them and start to see more downfield targets in order to land on the fantasy radar. The 24-year-old is only worth late-round consideration in deeper formats.

players.name: Kyle Williams
players.outlook: Williams showed some flashes as a rookie, but he has a tough path to a Year 2 breakout. The 2025 third-round pick didn't see the field much (31% snap share), but he scored three touchdowns on 21 targets, all of which came from Week 10 on. He was utilized as a situational deep threat (17.6 aDOT), which led to a low catch rate (48%) but plenty of big plays (20.9 YPR). That role may not change much in 2026, as Williams will now compete with newcomers A.J. Brown and Romeo Doubs, as well as incumbents Kayshon Boutte, DeMario Douglas and Mack Hollins for targets in a good New England offense led by Drake Maye. Unfortunately, he doesn't have a path to fantasy consistency and is best stashed only in dynasty.

players.name: Zavion Thomas
players.outlook: Thomas was selected by the Bears in the third round of April's draft. The LSU product is small (5'10/190), but extremely fast (4.28 40-yard dash) and figures to make his hay as a situational rusher, receiver and returner in the pros. He was limited to a 13% target share across his final three collegiate seasons (one at Mississippi State and two at LSU), having failed to clear 41 catches, 536 receiving yards or five touchdowns in any of the three campaigns. He'll battle Kalif Raymond and Jahdae Walker for work behind Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III. Thomas is unlikely to emerge into a reliable fantasy option.

players.name: Bryce Lance
players.outlook: Lance was selected by the Saints in the fourth round of April's draft. Trey's little brother is the lone wide receiver in this rookie class who didn't play a single snap at the FBS level. However, he was productive as a perimeter receiver at North Dakota State, posting receiving lines of 75-1,053-17 and 51-1,079-8 over the last two seasons. He's a year or so older than most of his classmates, but he dominated at the combine with terrific showings in the 40-yard dash (4.34), vertical (41.5) and broad jump (133). He has the size and skillset to battle Devaughn Vele for New Orleans' No. 3 gig behind Chris Olave and first-round rookie Jordyn Tyson. Lance's primary value is in dynasty leagues.

players.name: Brenen Thompson
players.outlook: Thompson was selected by the Chargers in the fourth round of April's draft. Thompson is an extremely small and very fast (4.26 40-yard dash) receiver who posted a rookie-class-high 18.5 YPR during his career and handled a massive 43% of Mississippi State's air yards last season. He didn't see much work prior to 2025, however, and figures to operate as a situational deep threat in the pros. He'll battle second-years Tre Harris and KeAndre Lamber-Smith for occasional looks behind Ladd McConkey and Quentin Johnston this season.

players.name: Kevin Coleman Jr.
players.outlook: Coleman Jr. was selected by the Dolphins in the fifth round of April's draft. Coleman is a small, slot receiver and punt returner who aligned inside on 83% of his 943 collegiate pass routes. Despite playing at three different FBS schools over the last three seasons, Coleman handled a solid 21% target share during the span and played well, catching 72% of his targets and dropping only four of 229 targets (1.7%). He posted a weak, 88 speed score at the combine, running a 4.49 40-yard dash at 179 pounds. Coleman will battle fellow rookies Chris Bell and Caleb Douglas, as well as a host of veterans for a role in a Miami offense likely to struggle to score points. He should only be drafted in dynasty.

players.name: CJ Daniels
players.outlook: Daniels was selected by the Rams in the sixth round of April's draft. It helped that he played in 68 games across six seasons at Liberty, LSU and Miami, but Daniels ranked third in this WR rookie class in receiving yards (2,991) and second in TD receptions (28) for his career. His overall efficiency was good, though he failed to emerge as anything more than a secondary target once he left Liberty. Daniels will battle for a roster spot and is no more than a dynasty stash.

players.name: Malik Benson
players.outlook: Benson was selected by the Raiders in the sixth round of April's draft. An accomplished sprinter, the speedy Benson has big play written all over him, though he's a slightly older prospect and his collegiate production was limited by a lot of changes in scenery (two years of community college followed by 2023 at Alabama, 2024 at Florida State and 2025 at Oregon). Benson never cleared a 15% target share in a single season, peaking with a 43-719-6 receiving line in 2025. Benson is a longshot as a sixth rounder, but the Raiders' shaky wide receiver depth chart presents him with an opportunity to quickly earn a role. Still, he's no more than a deep sleeper.

players.name: Deion Burks
players.outlook: Burks was selected by the Colts in the seventh round of April's draft. Burks is a small receiver who has experience as both a primary perimeter receiver (under 25% slot in 2023 and 2025) and a primary slot (over 80% slot in 2022 and 2024). He had productive seasons at both Purdue and Oklahoma, though his overall efficiency is fairly poor across the board. That includes an ugly 6.4 YPT and 1.37 YPRR, both of which rank near the basement of this rookie WR class. He showed very well athletically at the combine, including a 4.30 40-yard dash and class-best marks in the vertical (42.5) and bench press (26 reps). The No. 3 gig is there for the taking in Indianapolis, so consider Burks a deep sleeper.

players.name: Tyrell Shavers
players.outlook: Shavers tore his ACL in the playoffs and is unlikely to make a fantasy impact once back on the field in 2026.

players.name: Barion Brown
players.outlook: Brown was selected by the Saints in the sixth round of April's draft. Brown is a thin, vertical receiver who handled a healthy 21% target share across 50 collegiate games at Kentucky and LSU. Brown struggled a bit with drops and efficiency, but made some noise as a rusher (32-228-2 across four seasons) and figures to make his biggest impact as a kick returner (65 kick returns for 1,910 yards and six TDs are all easily tops in this rookie class). With Chris Olave and Jordyn Tyson cemented atop the depth chart, Brown is well off the fantasy radar.

players.name: Josh Cameron
players.outlook: Cameron was selected by the Jaguars in the sixth round of April's draft. Cameron is a big, strong, perimeter receiver who aligned out wide on 84% of his collegiate routes across five seasons at Baylor. Cameron's overall efficiency is underwhelming (1.58 YPRR), but he was certainly busy the last two seasons, totaling 52-754-10 and 69-872-9 receiving lines. He'll begin his career no higher than fifth on the Jacksonville depth chart behind Brian Thomas Jr., Jakobi Meyers, Parker Washington and Travis Hunter.

players.name: Reggie Virgil
players.outlook: Virgil was selected by the Cardinals in the fifth round of April's draft. Virgil is a tall, thin, perimeter, vertical receiver who ranked near the top of this WR rookie class in aDOT (14.7) and YPR (15.8) but near the basement in RAC (4.5), catch rate (54%) and YPRR (1.79) during his career at Miami Ohio and Texas Tech. A class-high 48% of his targets came on deep routes, though he played a more well-rounded role in 2025 -- his lone season with the Red Raiders. Virgil ran a 4.57 40-yard dash at 187 pounds at the combine, which works out to a poor speed score of 86. Virgil will battle Kendrick Bourne for No. 3 duties behind Marvin Harrison Jr. and Michael Wilson. He's off the short-term fantasy radar.

players.name: Kendrick Law
players.outlook: Law was selected by the Lions in the fifth round of April's draft. Law is a short-area/slot target who played a limited role in three seasons at Alabama (33 catches in 34 games) prior to delivering a 53-540-3 receiving line at Kentucky last season. Law's career 4.2 aDOT is lowest in this WR rookie class, but his 9.1 RAC and 78% catch rate rank first. He'll battle for a returner/depth receiver role and isn't a fantasy option.

players.name: Emmanuel Henderson Jr.
players.outlook: Henderson was selected by the Seahawks in the sixth round of April's draft. Henderson is a thin receiver with small hands who has experience playing inside and out (career 38% slot rate). A converted running back, Henderson was limited to five catches in three seasons at Alabama prior to making a leap to a 45-766-5 receiving line (22% target share) in his lone season at Kansas. At least in the short term, he'll be limited to special teams work.

players.name: Trey McBride
players.outlook: McBride has emerged as one of the league's top pass catchers -- not just tight ends -- as he trails only Ja'Marr Chase in targets (317) and receptions (252) over the past two seasons and is sixth in receiving yards (2,385). McBride led the tight end position in every fantasy-relevant counting stat in 2025, and his league-high 15 top-12 fantasy weeks were six more than any other player at the position. He finished third in the NFL with 17 end zone targets. McBride really thrived when Jacoby Brissett (21.1 PPG) replaced Kyler Murray (12.5) under center, which only adds to his 2026 appeal. The 26-year-old should be on your radar at the first-/second-round turn.

players.name: Brock Bowers
players.outlook: Bowers entered 2025 with high expectations and appeared well on his way to another productive season with 103 yards in Week 1, but a late-game injury limited him for three weeks and led to five full missed games. Bowers remained productive when healthy, finishing second among tight ends in fantasy PPG. Incredibly, he tied for fifth in the NFL with 13 end zone targets despite all the missed action. Bowers, who posted a 112-1,194-5 receiving line as a rookie in 2024, will be the top target for No. 1 pick Fernando Mendoza. The 23-year-old is well positioned for a highly productive season and should be on your radar in the second or third round.

players.name: Tyler Warren
players.outlook: Warren put together a solid rookie season, but it could've been even better. In 12 games with a healthy Daniel Jones, Warren scored five touchdowns and averaged 13.1 fantasy PPG. In five games without Jones, Warren fell to 6.3 PPG and failed to find the end zone. In fact, the 2025 first-round pick was unlucky in the scoring department, ranking fifth among tight ends in end zone targets (seven) and expected TDs (7.5). Warren finished the season third among tight ends in targets and also had three goal-line carries. The 24-year-old was already a featured target and might see even more work with Michael Pittman Jr. shipped to Pittsburgh. Warren is a quality TE1.

players.name: Colston Loveland
players.outlook: After averaging 4.1 targets and 8.5 fantasy PPG during his first 14 NFL games, Loveland leaped to 12.0 targets (10-plus in each) and 20.0 fantasy PPG during his final four games of 2025 (including the playoffs). For perspective, there have been only 12 instances of a tight end seeing at least 10 targets in four consecutive regular-season games in NFL history (and only one was a rookie). Loveland's overall efficiency was terrific, as he ranked eighth or better among tight ends in YPR, YPT and YPRR. With DJ Moore gone, the 2025 first-round pick enters his age-22 season all but locked into a massive workload alongside Rome Odunze and Luther Burden III. Loveland's ceiling is lofty, and midrange fantasy TE1 should be his floor.

players.name: Kyle Pitts Sr.
players.outlook: Right when it seemed like Pitts might never live up to the hype, the 2021 No. 4 pick put together the best season of his career. Pitts finished second among tight ends in routes, targets, catches, yards and fantasy points, setting career highs in all but the yardage category. He scored five touchdowns and has increased his total in that category (by exactly one) each season in the league. Pitts did a big chunk of his damage with Kirk Cousins under center (some with WR Drake London sidelined), which is notable as Tua Tagovailoa and Michael Penix Jr. are set to battle for the Atlanta QB job. There's some obvious risk here considering Pitts finished 17th or lower in PPG three straight seasons before 2025, but he nonetheless enters his age-25 season as a priority target in the Atlanta offense. Consider him a midrange TE1.

players.name: Harold Fannin Jr.
players.outlook: Fannin is a rising star at the tight end position after overcoming the presence of David Njoku and a quarterback merry-go-round to produce a 72-731-6 receiving line (plus 7-13-1 rushing) as a 21-year-old rookie last season. The third-round pick's 108 targets rank eighth all time by a rookie tight end, and he finished no lower than eighth at the position in catches, yards, touchdowns and fantasy points last season. Fannin figures to see some touchdown regression (seven TDs, 3.8 expected, three end zone targets) and Cleveland still has quarterback question marks, but with Njoku gone, Fannin has a path to improve a bit on what was already a healthy 22% target share. He's a solid TE1 target.

players.name: Sam LaPorta
players.outlook: LaPorta has settled in as one of the league's top tight ends, but the 2023 second-round pick simply hasn't been able to match the fantasy success he enjoyed as a rookie. Since posting an 86-889-10 receiving line in 17 games that first season, LaPorta has totaled a 100-1,215-10 line in 25 games over the past two seasons. LaPorta's 2025 campaign was cut short by a back injury, otherwise he was well on his way to his third straight top-10 fantasy campaign (he finished seventh in PPG). The 25-year-old joins Amon-Ra St. Brown, Jameson Williams and Jahmyr Gibbs as the clear "Core Four" targets in a good, pass-friendly Detroit offense, and is thus a solid fantasy TE1.

players.name: George Kittle
players.outlook: Kittle is undoubtedly among the best tight ends in the NFL and fantasy, but he simply can't dodge the injury bug. The 2017 fifth-round pick has played one full season (2018) and tore an Achilles in the team's wild-card victory after missing six regular-season games last season. Kittle is likely to miss at least one month to open the season and, now at 32 years old, might not be his usual, dominant self even when he returns. Kittle has finished sixth or better in fantasy PPG in each of the past eight seasons (including first in 2024), so he's worth a late-round flier, especially if you have an available IR slot.

players.name: Travis Kelce
players.outlook: Kelce re-signed with the Chiefs and is returning for what might be his final NFL season. The veteran tight end's days as an elite producer are behind him, and he saw a notable dip in usage last season (19.6% target share was his lowest since 2014). Kelce did hold up for 17 games and still finished in the top 10 at the position in snaps, routes, targets, catches, yards and fantasy PPG. Kelce has reeled off 10 consecutive top-five fantasy campaigns despite not offering much in the scoring department (he hasn't cleared five TDs in a season since 2022). The 36-year-old's role could dip even further this season, so he's best valued as a fringe TE1.

players.name: Dallas Goedert
players.outlook: Goedert enters the 2026 season as the poster boy for inevitable touchdown regression to the mean. After scoring 24 touchdowns in his first seven seasons (3.4 per year), Goedert inexplicably leaped to 11 scores in 15 games in 2025. Goedert's expected TD total was 5.1, and he saw only five end zone targets (12 tight ends saw more). Goedert is a solid fantasy tight end -- he has finished 12th or better in PPG each of the past seven seasons -- but he often misses games (at least two in each of the past six years) and has never finished in the top eight at the position in targets or catches. The 31-year-old is likely to deliver fringe-TE1 numbers in 2026.

players.name: Jake Ferguson
players.outlook: Ferguson has emerged as a reliable target for Dak Prescott in the high-scoring Dallas offense, which has led to solid fantasy production. Ferguson, who has handled a 17% target share each of the past three seasons, is fresh off a career year in which he ranked third among tight ends in receptions, touchdowns and end zone targets (10). His nine top-12 fantasy weeks tied for second, though it's worth noting that his production dropped off substantially with a healthy CeeDee Lamb and George Pickens on the field during the second half of the season (under 12.0 fantasy points in each of his final eight healthy games, 7.3 PPG during the span). The 27-year-old's role as a short-range target (4.7 aDOT last season) limits his yardage upside, but he sees just enough work, including near the goal line, to allow fringe-TE1 production. Don't call him "Joke Farterson" ("Fantasy Football Now" viewers get it); Ferguson is vying for his third top-10 fantasy campaign in his past four seasons.

players.name: Tucker Kraft
players.outlook: Kraft's steady career progression had him on the cusp of a full-on breakout last season, but all was derailed by a torn ACL in Week 9. Prior to the injury, Kraft had seen 10 targets in consecutive games, one of which included a career-high 33.3 fantasy points. Through Week 8, only Jake Ferguson had more fantasy points. Granted, Kraft was way over his head in the TD department (six TDs with an expected total of 1.8 and only two end zone targets), but his boost in usage and terrific RAC skills (he has led the position in the category each of his three seasons) were enough to get him into the weekly TE1 mix. Kraft's recovery will need to be monitored, but even if he's ready for Week 1, a dip in production is possible in his first year back from the major injury. He's a risky middle-round target.

players.name: Isaiah Likely
players.outlook: Likely signed with the Giants after four years of backing up Mark Andrews in Baltimore. Likely is reuniting with coach John Harbaugh and is finally expected to play a consistent offensive role. During his time in Baltimore, Likely was limited to 50% of the team's offensive snaps and never finished a season better than 20th among tight ends in snaps, routes, targets, catches, yards or fantasy PPG. His only extensive stretch with Andrews sidelined came back in 2023. During the seven-game span, Likely posted a 23-356-6 receiving line on a 16% target share and averaged a solid 13.5 fantasy PPG. The 26-year-old is obviously unproven, but his receiving skills in New York's ascending offense are enough to put him on the TE2 radar.

players.name: Mark Andrews
players.outlook: One of fantasy's biggest mysteries, Andrews' usage has dipped substantially over the past two seasons. After finishing in the top five in fantasy PPG five seasons in a row from 2019 to 2023, Andrews slipped to eighth in 2024 (it would've been much worse if not for 11 touchdowns on 68 targets) and fell even further to 24th last season. Andrews appeared in all 17 of Baltimore's games but ranked 18th among tight ends in targets and outside the top 20 in catches and yards. The combination of a new coaching staff, a healthy Lamar Jackson and the departure of Isaiah Likely could lead to a rebound season for Andrews, but we now have two straight seasons of underperformance. The 31-year-old remains a force near the goal line (top 10 in end zone targets seven years in a row), but he's now more of a fringe TE1 than a fantasy star.

players.name: Kenyon Sadiq
players.outlook: Sadiq was selected by the Jets with the No. 16 pick of April's draft. After playing a limited role his first two collegiate seasons, he broke out with a 51-560-8 receiving line in 2025. He handled a respectable 17% of Oregon's targets and aligned in the slot 62% of the time. Sadiq is a bit on the small side, but he totaled 106 targets in three collegiate seasons. He needs to cut down on drops (six on 70 targets in 2025), but he's one of the youngest players in this rookie class and crushed it at the combine, posting a ridiculous 4.39 40-yard dash at 241 pounds (130 speed score) and a 43.5-inch vertical, 133-inch broad jump and 26 bench reps. Sadiq's career might start slow, especially with Mason Taylor also on the Jets' TE depth chart, but the rookie has the upside to eventually emerge as a fantasy TE1. Consider him with a mid-to-late pick.

players.name: T.J. Hockenson
players.outlook: It's been a rough go for Hockenson since he peaked with a 95-960-5 receiving line in 2023. He tore an ACL in Week 16 that season and was limited to a 41-455-0 receiving line upon his return in 10 games in 2024. A bounce-back was expected in 2025, but Hockenson handled a weak 16% target share (his lowest since he was a rookie in 2019) and couldn't overcome the team's QB woes en route to a 51-438-3 showing. Hockenson has now followed four straight top-seven fantasy PPG seasons with finishes of 17th and 26th. The 29-year-old could very well get back on track in 2026, but he'll need solid play from either Kyler Murray or J.J. McCarthy. Hockenson is a fringe TE1.

players.name: Dalton Kincaid
players.outlook: Kincaid is perhaps fantasy's biggest wild card among tight ends. The 2023 first-round pick has occasionally flashed a high ceiling (he actually led qualified tight ends in YPT and YPRR last season), but he also has missed substantial action (nine games the past two years) and has disappeared way too often (he failed to clear 13.1 fantasy points in any game in 2024, and never cleared six targets in a game in 2025). Kincaid was limited to a 37% snap share when active last season, and he has yet to finish a season better than 14th in fantasy PPG. The 26-year-old has TE1 upside, but he's tough to trust and has added target competition (WR DJ Moore) in 2026. Consider Kincaid only in the mid-to-late rounds.

players.name: Hunter Henry
players.outlook: Henry has settled in as one of Drake Maye's top targets, having reached 85 targets, 60 catches and 650 yards each of the past two seasons (each ranked in the top 10 among tight ends). A threat downfield and near the goal line, Henry has finished in the top 15 in aDOT each of the past seven seasons and 12th or better in end zone targets in seven of his nine campaigns. Despite the solid usage, Henry hasn't sported much fantasy upside, as he has failed to finish in the top 12 in fantasy PPG since 2020 (he has never finished better than eighth). The 31-year-old has added target competition this season (Romeo Doubs and A.J. Brown), so he's no more than a fringe TE2.

players.name: Juwan Johnson
players.outlook: Johnson's impressive progression continued in 2025, as the converted wide receiver posted career-high marks in targets, catches, yardage and fantasy points (all four were eighth or better among tight ends). Johnson produced nine top-12 fantasy weeks, which tied for second at the position. He has finished in the top 12 in aDOT each of the past four seasons but hasn't been much of an option near the goal line (no more than four TDs or end zone targets in each of the past three seasons). The 29-year-old has added target competition, including eighth-overall pick Jordyn Tyson, but he very well could settle in as high as third in target priority in the Saints' Tyler Shough-led offense. Johnson is a fine TE2.

players.name: Oronde Gadsden
players.outlook: Gadsden's rookie season was quite the roller coaster. The 2025 fifth-round pick didn't get much early run prior to exploding for 29.4 fantasy points in Week 7 and 18.7 points in Week 8. He went on to score one touchdown and average 6.2 fantasy PPG the rest of the season, never clearing 12.2 points in a single outing. Gadsden finished the season eighth or better at the position in end zone targets, aDOT, YPR, YPT and YPRR. The 23-year-old's second-half fall is concerning, and he now enters Year 2 set to compete for work with newcomer David Njoku. It's still possible Gadsden emerges as a featured target in new playcaller Mike McDaniel's offense, but consistent fantasy output may be tough in an offense that figures to spread the ball around. Gadsden is only worth a late pick in deeper leagues.

players.name: Brenton Strange
players.outlook: Strange played well in his first season as Jacksonville's clear No. 1 tight end, but it simply didn't lead to much fantasy success. The 2023 second-round pick handled a solid 16% target share during the 12 games he was healthy, but that was only enough to lead to 9.8 fantasy PPG (18th among tight ends). Strange simply hasn't been utilized much near the goal line, as he's yet to clear three touchdowns or four end zone targets in a single season. The 25-year-old is a reliable target for QB Trevor Lawrence, but with the team so well-stocked at wide receiver, consistent fantasy production is a long shot. Consider Strange a low-ceiling TE2.

players.name: Chig Okonkwo
players.outlook: Okonkwo signed with the Commanders after spending his first four seasons in Tennessee. The 2022 fourth-round pick is one of only four tight ends who have appeared in all 68 games since he entered the league, and he's also one of only six tight ends who have registered 50-plus receptions each of the past three seasons. On the other hand, Okonkwo has yet to clear a 16% target share in a single season, and he has been a nonfactor near the goal line (eight TDs and 11 end zone targets in his career). As a result, he has finished exactly 21st in fantasy points each of the past three seasons. Perhaps he'll make a leap as one of Jayden Daniels' top targets, but the 27-year-old is no more than a back-end TE2 lottery ticket.

players.name: Dalton Schultz
players.outlook: Schultz has been one of the league's most consistent tight ends over the past six years, but with the exception of one season, he hasn't been overly useful in fantasy. Schultz was fantasy's No. 3 tight end in scoring while he was in Dallas in 2021, but he has finished 10th or worse in his other eight campaigns (that includes exactly 10th in three of the past four seasons). In 2025, Schultz ranked sixth or better at the position in targets, catches and yards, but he found the end zone only three times and has now cleared five touchdowns once in his career (eight in 2021). Schultz is a reliable target for QB C.J. Stroud, but with Houston's depth at wide receiver, he simply doesn't offer much upside. Consider him a low-ceiling TE2.

players.name: Pat Freiermuth
players.outlook: Freiermuth's career trajectory has been quite the head-scratcher, but the bottom line is he is yet to emerge as a consistent fantasy starter. The 2021 second-round pick flirted with TE1 status in both 2022 (11th in fantasy PPG) and 2024 (14th) but saw a dramatic dip in usage in Pittsburgh's crowded tight end room in 2025. Freiermuth posted a career-low 10% target share and finished outside the top 20 at the position in snaps, routes, targets, catches, yards and end zone targets, and 30 tight ends bested his 6.7 fantasy PPG. The 27-year-old figures to play more in 2026 with Jonnu Smith gone and Mike McCarthy running the offense, but Pittsburgh is also better at wide receiver with Michael Pittman Jr. and Germie Bernard in the fold. Freiermuth has some sleeper appeal, but he's best valued as a back-end TE2.

players.name: Terrance Ferguson
players.outlook: Ferguson is a potential breakout candidate in his second NFL season. As expected, the 2025 second-rounder played a situational role behind the likes of Tyler Higbee, Colby Parkinson and Davis Allen as a rookie. He didn't get much run, but when he did, he was used as a vertical threat to the extreme. His 17.5 aDOT is highest by any tight end (minimum 10 targets) in at least a decade. The Rams are returning the same tight end room plus second-round pick Max Klare, so while consistent production could prove elusive, Ferguson very well could emerge as the No. 3 pass catcher behind Puka Nacua and 33-year-old Davante Adams in the Rams' elite offense. The 23-year-old, who also saw five end zone targets as a rookie, is worth a late-round lottery ticket.

players.name: David Njoku
players.outlook: Njoku signed with the Chargers after spending his first nine NFL seasons in Cleveland. The 2017 first-round pick was a late bloomer and has struggled with durability (he hasn't played a full season since 2018), but he has been a productive fantasy starter in recent years. Prior to a lost 2025 campaign, Njoku finished in the top eight among tight ends in fantasy PPG three seasons in a row. A force near the goal line, Njoku paced the position in end zone targets in both 2023 and 2024. At 30 years old, Njoku likely still has something left in the tank, but he has a tough path to fantasy relevance in a Los Angeles tight end room that also includes ascending Oronde Gadsden II. Njoku will only be worth a late flier if he quickly takes control as the team's clear starter at the position.

players.name: AJ Barner
players.outlook: Barner was a solid contributor for the Seattle offense last season. The 2024 fourth-round pick worked his way to the top of the depth chart, and although his overall volume was limited (20th among TEs in targets and yards), he found the end zone seven times and finished 14th in fantasy points (21st in PPG). The 24-year-old is a serviceable, short-range target for Sam Darnold, but even with some rushing worked in, his role in Seattle's low-volume passing offense won't allow much upside. In fact, it's possible he loses some targets to second-year TE Elijah Arroyo this season. Barner is a fringe TE2, at best.

players.name: Gunnar Helm
players.outlook: Helm quietly caught 44 passes during his rookie season, which -- believe it or not -- ranks 11th among rookie tight ends over the past decade. Impressively, the 2025 fourth-round pick hit that mark despite playing second fiddle to starter Chig Okonkwo. Helm, who caught a healthy 79% of his 56 targets, wasn't much of a fantasy option as a rookie (5.7 PPG), but with Okonkwo out of the picture, he's positioned for a much larger role in his second season. Helm will need a leap from QB Cam Ward, but if that happens, a breakout season is possible. The 24-year-old is worth a late flier in deeper leagues.

players.name: Evan Engram
players.outlook: Engram returns for his second season in Denver after an underwhelming debut campaign with the team. The veteran tight end was limited to a career-low 13% target share and finished 15th or lower among tight ends in routes, targets, catches and yardage, despite appearing in 16 games. Engram worked as a short-area target but still struggled to a poor 68% catch rate and was, as usual, a nonfactor near the goal line (one TD, two end zone targets). Engram hasn't cleared four touchdowns in a season since he scored six as a rookie in 2017. He finished 32nd among tight ends in fantasy PPG and isn't a good bet for a larger role this season, especially with Jaylen Waddle added to the offense. The 32-year-old Engram is a fantasy TE3, at best.

players.name: Jake Tonges
players.outlook: Tonges enters his fourth NFL season very much on the fantasy radar due to a torn Achilles suffered by George Kittle during last season's playoffs. Kittle is expected to return at some point this season, but while he's out, Tonges is set to replace him as the 49ers' top pass catcher at the position. Kittle missed seven full games last season, including the playoffs (Weeks 2-6, 17, 20). During those games, Tonges handled a 17.9% target share (6.6 per game) and posted a 34-328-3 receiving line. That works out to 11.8 fantasy PPG, which would've ranked eighth among all tight ends last season, ahead of the likes of Harold Fannin Jr., Travis Kelce, Tyler Warren and Jake Ferguson. Tonges will be a fringe TE1 as long as Kittle is sidelined and is thus worth late-round consideration.

players.name: Eli Stowers
players.outlook: Stowers was selected by the Eagles in the second round of April's draft. Stowers is a converted quarterback who improved his production each of the last three seasons, peaking with a 62-769-4 receiving line in 2025. He aligned in the slot on 72% of his collegiate routes (highest among this rookie tight end class) and his career 2.38 YPRR is easily tops in this TE class. He's not much of a blocker, but he sports good speed and athleticism (he posted the best vertical and broad jumps among TEs at the combine). Stowers will have minimal fantasy value while working behind Dallas Goedert as a rookie, but he has long-term TE1 upside and will be a popular breakout candidate in 2027.

players.name: Greg Dulcich
players.outlook: Dulcich is back on the fantasy radar as the top tight end on the Miami depth chart. The 2022 third-round pick for the Denver Broncos made some noise as a rookie with a 33-411-2 receiving line in 10 games, but he went on to total 16 targets during the 2023 and 2024 seasons before landing in Miami last season. Dulcich didn't see his first target until Week 9 but went on to post a 26-335-1 receiving line the rest of the way. He wasn't a regular fantasy option (one top-15 week among TEs), but he certainly has a path to a substantial target share in 2026 with minimal competition at tight end and with Miami so short-handed at wide receiver. The 26-year-old is a long shot for consistent fantasy value in an offense expected to struggle, but there's some deep-league sleeper appeal.

players.name: Cade Otton
players.outlook: Otton re-signed with Tampa Bay after spending his first four NFL seasons with the team. Since entering the league in 2022, Otton ranks first among all tight ends in snaps (3,530) and second in routes (1,838). That simply hasn't translated to much target volume (he is yet to clear 86 in a season) or goal-line work (11 TDs and 19 end zone targets in 63 games). In fact, Otton was the only player with 75-plus targets who didn't score multiple touchdowns last season. The 27-year-old has finished 25th or lower in fantasy PPG in three of four seasons and is a poor bet for his first top-10 finish in 2026. He's well off the fantasy radar.

players.name: Mike Gesicki
players.outlook: There was an era when Gesicki was a viable starting option in fantasy, but that time has come and gone. Since posting back-to-back top-eight fantasy campaigns in 2020 and 2021, Gesicki has finished 23rd, 37th, 13th and 41st the past four seasons. The latter came during a 2025 campaign in which he was limited to 41 targets in 13 games. Gesicki is no more than a situational seam stretcher and not much of an option near the goal line (he has exactly two TDs and five end zone targets each of the past three seasons). The 30-year-old will be a risky streaming option even if Ja'Marr Chase or Tee Higgins misses time.

players.name: Darnell Washington
players.outlook: Though he never quite made it onto the fantasy radar, Washington was one of the most fun, surprising �breakout� players of 2025. Essentially an extension of the offensive line at 6-foot-6, 264 pounds, Washington is known mainly for his blocking. Still, the 2023 third-round pick played well enough that he was stealing snaps and targets from both Jonnu Smith and Pat Freiermuth. Washington never cleared six targets or 10.7 fantasy points in any game, but he was effective with the ball in his hands and averaged 8.5 yards per target. Though Smith has been released, Pittsburgh's offense is no longer being led by TE superfan Arthur Smith, which is likely to lead to a lesser role for Washington. Expect him to operate as a rarely targeted blocking complement to Freiermuth.

players.name: Colby Parkinson
players.outlook: Parkinson is set to resume his role in the Rams' TE committee. The 2020 fourth-round pick was a part-time player early last season, but graduated to a much larger role in the second half. In fact, from Week 10 on (12 games, including the playoffs), Parkinson averaged 5.0 targets and 12.1 fantasy points per game, scoring nine touchdowns along the way. He finished the regular season with career-high marks across the board, which included his first top-35 fantasy campaign (17th). If Parkinson carries his increased role into 2026, he'll be a sneaky fantasy option, but the problem is that he needs to fend off Tyler Higbee and recent second-round picks Terrance Ferguson and Max Klare for work in an offense that also includes Puka Nacua and Davante Adams. Parkinson, 27, is a deep sleeper worth a look in leagues that start two tight ends.

players.name: Theo Johnson
players.outlook: Johnson was a solid find by the Giants in the fourth round of the 2024 draft, but he's headed for a reduced role this season following New York's signing of Isaiah Likely and Malik Nabers' return to health. The change comes after Johnson ranked top 12 among tight ends in both snaps and routes, but 23rd in catches and 19th in yards last season. Johnson was busy near the goal line (seven end zone targets), but struggled with drops (tight end-high nine), which led to a poor 63% catch rate. Johnson finished 23rd in fantasy PPG and now may be lucky to see a target or two per game. He's well off the fantasy radar.

players.name: Dawson Knox
players.outlook: Knox enters his eighth season as a solid role player for the Buffalo offense. He is on the field quite a bit (20th among tight ends in snaps last season), but defers to Dalton Kincaid when it comes to the team's top target priority at the TE position. Even when Kincaid was sidelined last season, Knox was limited to only 3.6 targets and 6.1 fantasy points per game. Knox has never posted a top-10 fantasy campaign and has finished outside the top 30 in PPG each of the last three seasons. Even if Kincaid misses additional time, Knox will max out as a fantasy TE2.

players.name: Mason Taylor
players.outlook: Taylor was a busy man during his rookie season, playing 81% of the Jets' offensive snaps and handling a healthy 19% of the targets in 13 games. The 2025 second-round pick struggled badly with efficiency while dealing with the team's quarterback woes, ranking near the basement in catch rate (65%) and yards per target (5.4). Taylor also dropped five passes (fourth most), scored only one touchdown and finished 30th in fantasy PPG. Taylor would've been a potential 2026 breakout candidate, but that was quickly derailed when the Jets selected Kenyon Sadiq in the first round of April's draft. Taylor will play a role in the New York offense, but he won't see nearly enough targets to allow any sort of fantasy relevance.

players.name: Michael Mayer
players.outlook: Mayer enters his first contract year expected to resume his role as Las Vegas' oft-utilized No. 2 TE behind Brock Bowers. The 2023 second-round pick has yet to come close to playing a full season, nor has he emerged as a reliable fantasy option. Last season, he averaged 2.9 targets and 3.4 fantasy points in nine games with Bowers, compared to 6.0 targets and 10.9 PPG in the four games in which Bowers was sidelined. A short-range target (career 5.5 aDOT) with minimal goal line work (one career end zone target), Mayer is way off the fantasy radar. He should be added to rosters only if Bowers misses time.

players.name: Elijah Arroyo
players.outlook: Arroyo is eyeing a Year 2 breakout after an underwhelming rookie campaign in which he played a clear second fiddle to A.J. Barner. The 2025 second-round pick was limited to 39% of the offensive snaps during the 13 games he was active. He cleared three targets in a game only once (five in Week 7) and his lone touchdown came on one of just two targets in Week 9. Arroyo was effective as a vertical target at the collegiate level, so he remains an intriguing dynasty hold, but the 23-year-old has a tough path to short-term fantasy relevance while stuck behind Barner in Seattle's low-pass-volume offense.

players.name: Ja'Tavion Sanders
players.outlook: Sanders is back as Carolina's top pass-catching tight end, though he'll need a larger share of the target pie if he hopes to work his way onto the fantasy radar. The 2024 fourth-round pick flashed as a rookie with a 33-342-1 receiving line while playing 55% of Carolina's offensive snaps and handling 9.1% of the targets across 16 games. He simply didn't progress in Year 2, posting a 29-190-1 line while playing 52% of the snaps and handling a 10% target share in 12 healthy games. Sanders has been an inefficient short-range target with little goal line activity thus far (four career end zone targets), placing the 23-year-old well off the fantasy radar.

players.name: Max Klare
players.outlook: Klare was selected by the Rams in the second round of April's draft. Klare is a receiving tight end who has experience playing in line and in the slot. He might not offer much as a blocker in the pros, but he showed his receiving ability at two different schools, posting a 51-685-4 line with Purdue in 2024 and a 43-448-2 line with Ohio State in 2025. Klare has the speed and athleticism to eventually emerge as a fantasy option, but hell begin his career in a crowded tight end room that also includes Colby Parkinson, Tyler Higbee, Davis Allen and 2025 second-round pick Terrance Ferguson. Klare is nowhere close to fantasy relevance at the moment.

players.name: Tyler Higbee
players.outlook: Higbee re-signed with the Rams and will battle for work with the likes of Colby Parkinson, Davis Allen, Terrance Ferguson and rookie Max Klare. The 2016 fourth-round pick has a pair of top-10 fantasy campaigns in his career, but the last one came in 2022 and he's finished 24th or worse in three consecutive seasons. Injuries have been part of the issue (21 missed games over the last two seasons), but so has a lesser role and minimal goal-line work (seven touchdowns and four end zone targets over the last three years). Now 33 years old, Higbee is likely third in line for targets at his position on his own team and, thus, nowhere close to fantasy relevance.

players.name: Eli Raridon
players.outlook: Raridon was selected by the Patriots in the third round of April's draft. Raridon is a tall, blocking tight end who was barely targeted (5% share) and totaled a 48-623-3 receiving line during four seasons at Notre Dame. Most of the receiving work came in 2025, so perhaps there's some hope in that area, but he's a long shot for fantasy value any time soon, especially with Hunter Henry and likely Julian Hill ahead of him on the depth chart this season.

players.name: Oscar Delp
players.outlook: Delp was selected by the Saints in the third round of April's draft. Delp was busy during his final three seasons at Georgia (1,733 snaps in 42 games), but that didn't lead to much work in the passing game (no more than 24 catches, 284 yards or a 7% target share in a single season). Good speed and hands suggest there's some hope for him as a developmental piece, which is what he'll be in 2026 while playing a situational role behind Juwan Johnson and Noah Fant.

players.name: Justin Joly
players.outlook: Joly was selected by the Broncos in the fifth round of April's draft. Joly is a highly productive receiving tight end who ranked near the top of this rookie TE class in all receiving counting stats (166-1,978-15) and target share (17%) during 50 collegiate games at UConn and NC State. He's not much of a blocker, but he could stretch the seam as a move tight end in the pros. Joly may one day emerge as a receiving option in Denver, but that's unlikely to happen this year with Evan Engram atop the depth chart.

players.name: Riley Nowakowski
players.outlook: Nowakowski was selected by the Steelers in the fifth round of April's draft. Nowakowski is a bit undersized with very small hands, but he's a capable blocker and, after totaling 18 catches in 36 games at Wisconsin, made a little bit of a leap as a pass catcher in 2025 (his lone season at Indiana), posting a 32-387-2 receiving line. He's expected to play fullback and offer some tight end depth behind Pat Freiermuth and Darnell Washington.

players.name: Sam Roush
players.outlook: Roush was selected by the Bears in the third round of April's draft. Roush is a huge, in-line blocking tight end. Though he did catch 40-plus passes each of his final two seasons at Stanford (including a healthy 22% target share in 2025), his overall receiving efficiency was underwhelming (6.6 YPT, 1.22 YPRR) and he totaled four TDs on 182 career targets. He's unlikely to see enough target volume in the pros to find his way to the fantasy radar and that's especially the case in Chicago, as he's buried behind Colston Loveland and Cole Kmet.

players.name: Marlin Klein
players.outlook: Klein was selected by the Texans in the second round of April's draft. He's an inexperienced, blocking tight end who posted an ugly 5.5 YPT and 57.6% catch rate in his collegiate career. Klein peaked with a 24-248-1 receiving line in 2025 and now joins a Texans' offense that already includes Dalton Schultz, Cade Stover and Foster Moreau at the tight end position. Klein may be Houston's tight end of the future, but he won't be on the fantasy radar as a rookie.

players.name: Will Kacmarek
players.outlook: Kacmarek was selected by the Dolphins in the third round of April's draft. Kacmarek is a massive, strong blocking tight end who won't see much receiving work in the pros. He's likely to play a lot of snap as a rookie considering Miami's shaky roster, but Greg Dulcich is expected to operate as the team's top pass-catcher at the tight end position. Kacmarek is no more than the deepest of deep sleepers.

players.name: Nate Boerkircher
players.outlook: Boerkircher was selected by the Jaguars in the second round of April's draft. Boerkircher is a blocking tight end who handled a 3% target share (lowest in this rookie TE class) across 52 collegiate games (five seasons). He aligned in the slot 30% of the time and posted a 1.04 YPRR, both of which are lowest in this TE group. He'll file in as a blocking complement to starter Brenton Strange and is not a fantasy option.

players.name: Tanner Koziol
players.outlook: Koziol was selected by the Jaguars in the fifth round of April's draft. Across 49 collegiate games at Houston and Ball State, Koziol produced 349 targets, 237 receptions, 2,234 yards, 24 TDs, a 23% target share and a 24% air yard share. All six stats easily top this TE rookie class and that production included a massive 94-839-8 showing in 2024. Koziol has work to do as a blocker, but his size and production suggest there's clear receiving upside. He's worth stashing in dynasty, but doesn't have a path to short-term value behind Brenton Strange and likely fellow rookie Nick Boerkircher.

players.name: Joe Royer
players.outlook: Royer was selected by the Browns in the fifth round of April's draft. After barely seeing the field during three seasons at Ohio State, Royer broke out with a 50-522-3 receiving line (21% target share) in 2024, prior to dipping to 29-416-4 (10%) in 2025. He has solid size with big hands and could emerge as a versatile depth option. He'll compete with a handful of veterans for Cleveland's No. 2 gig behind Harold Fannin Jr. this season.

players.name: Josh Cuevas
players.outlook: Cuevas was selected by the Ravens in the fifth round of April's draft. Cuevas wasn't targeted much during three collegiate seasons at Washington and Alabama (57-793-6, 5% target share), but he was effective when called on, averaging 10.4 yards per target. He's a bit of a tweener and underwhelmed athletically at the combine, but can help as a blocker and receiver. He'll battle fellow rookie Matthew Hibner for snaps behind Mark Andrews and Durham Smythe this season.

players.name: Jack Endries
players.outlook: Endries was selected by the Bengals in the seventh round of April's draft. Endries is a pass-catching/move tight end who caught 33-plus passes during each of his three collegiate seasons. He peaked with a 56-623-3 receiving line with Fernando Mendoza under center at Cal in 2024 prior to seeing less work in an in-line role at Texas last season. Endries will compete for the No. 3 gig in Cincinnati and isn't close to fantasy relevance.

players.name: Dallen Bentley
players.outlook: Bentley was selected by the Broncos in the seventh round of April's draft. Bentley is a big, in-line tight end who, after catching three passes during his first two seasons (18 games) at Utah, exploded for a 23% target share and 48-620-6 receiving line in 2025 (13 games). Bentley figures to focus primarily on blocking in the pros, but his 2025 pass-catching work suggests there's some hope for fantasy relevance in future seasons.
